coredata.dc:title: Construction of dual emissive nanoprobes based on carbon dots and their ratiometric sensing applications

Dong, Yuanyuan

Zhao, Jiaxiang
Wang, Guiling
Li, Zhaoqing
Fu, Qiuyue
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional functional carbon nanomaterials with multicolor emission characteristics. At present, many optical sensing systems based on CDs can work at a single wavelength. However, under single wavelength operating conditions, many factors such as sensor concentration, uniformity, instrument status, and changes in environmental conditions often affect the accuracy of the results. In contrast, the dual emission center can overcome these challenges, and improve the sensitivity and accuracy of sensing detection by using multi-dimensional signal output. In this review, we focus on exploring the fabrication techniques and intrinsic properties of CDs in detail. In addition, we also studied the construction process and practical application of optical sensors based on dual emission CDs. Finally, based on the current research level and actual needs, the future development of dual emissive CDs is prospected.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Carbon dots
Dual emission
Ratiometric sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of dual emissive materials based on CDs
Physical and optical properties of CDs
The role of CDs in dual emissive probes
Dual emission composites based on CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Classification of CDs: including GQDs, CQDs, CPDs, CNDs and CNQDs [11,13,14], (Copyright 2019 Wiley; Copyright 2021 Elsevier; Copyright 2023 Elsevier).

(A) Visualizing gaseous nitrogen dioxide by ratiometric PL of CNDs-quantum dots hybrid [21], (Copyright 2015 American Chemical Society); (B) Ratiometric dual emission of Rhodamine-B grafted CDs for full-range solvent components detection [23], (Copyright 2021 Elsevier); (C) Based on aggregation-induced enhanced emission CDs for ratiometric quantitative sensing of fluoride ions [27], (Copyright 2023 American Chemical Society).

(A) TEM image and corresponding AFM image of GQDs [37], (Copyright 2018 American Chemical Society); (B) TEM image of CPDs [38], (Copyright 2019 Royal Society of Chemistry); (C) TEM image and corresponding Raman spectrum of CQDs [39], (Copyright 2017 Wiley); (D) TEM image and corresponding XRD pattern [40], (Copyright 2016 American Chemical Society).

(A) CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites for ratiometric PL detection of Cu2+ [50], (Copyright 2020 Springer); (B) A ratiometric PL sensor based on CDs and QDs for visual detection of biogenic amines [52], (Copyright 2022 Elsevier).

(A) Dual emission of CDs@Eu-MOFs for ratiometric PL detection of Cr(VI) [53], (Copyright 2019 Elsevier); (B) Preparation of m-CD@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ for visual and detection of hypochlorite [54], (Copyright 2017 American Chemical Society).

(A) Dual emission ratiometric PL detection of dopamine based on CDs-AuNCs [55], (Copyright 2018 Elsevier); (B) Dual emission CDs for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ and acid phosphatase [57], (Copyright 2020 Elsevier).
Dual emissive nanoprobes based on CDs for their PL properties, role, detection target, linear range and LOD.

simple-head.ce:title: Construction of dual emissive nanoprobes based on carbon dots and their ratiometric sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Heilongjiang Institute of Technology, Harbin 150050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Applied Chemistry, Saga University, 1-Honjo, Saga 840-8502, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Applied Chemistry, Saga University, 1-Honjo, Saga 840-8502, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional functional carbon nanomaterials with multicolor emission characteristics. At present, many optical sensing systems based on CDs can work at a single wavelength. However, under single wavelength operating conditions, many factors such as sensor concentration, uniformity, instrument status, and changes in environmental conditions often affect the accuracy of the results. In contrast, the dual emission center can overcome these challenges, and improve the sensitivity and accuracy of sensing detection by using multi-dimensional signal output. In this review, we focus on exploring the fabrication techniques and intrinsic properties of CDs in detail. In addition, we also studied the construction process and practical application of optical sensors based on dual emission CDs. Finally, based on the current research level and actual needs, the future development of dual emissive CDs is prospected.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials exhibit a wide range of structures and morphologies, making them highly versatile for various applications [1]. These materials possess exceptional biocompatibility, stable chemical properties, large specific surface areas, and outstanding electrical conductivity. Hence, carbon nanomaterials become an important part of advancing various scientific and technological frontiers due to their unique characteristics. Currently, there has been significant advancement in the use of zero-dimensional fullerene, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes, two-dimensional graphene, and other carbon nanomaterials in various fields including materials science, life science, and chemistry. Owing to these advantageous properties, carbon nanomaterials have been widely used in drug delivery, sensing technology, catalysis, energy storage and other fields [2,3]. However, the above carbon nanomaterials are not efficient optical emission devices, and have limitations such as poor stability, lack of precise control of their size, large-scale production, and high cost. As a result, there is a growing research focus on the development of new carbon nanomaterials to address these limitations, and further expand their potential applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional functional carbon nanomaterials that exhibit multicolor emission properties [4–6]. Since Xu et al. first reported the discovery of CDs in 2004 [7], they have attracted the interest of many researchers due to their excellent physical and chemical properties, especially their outstanding absorption and photoluminescence (PL) properties. Therefore, they are widely used in chemical and biological sensing, cell imaging, bio-labeling, and other fields [8–10]. The structures of CDs are usually composed of sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon cores, and their surfaces have many functional groups and polymer chains. It is worth noting that, based on the difference of carbon core microstructure, specific subclasses of CDs have emerged recently, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) [11,12]. Recently, some reports have used graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) as a kind of CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 [13].

Currently, many optical sensing systems based on CDs operate at a single wavelength [15]. In order to enhance the detection sensitivity of optical sensors based on CDs, it is often necessary to utilize special element doping or implement surface modification techniques. These approaches are effective in improving the performance and accuracy of the sensing systems, enabling them to detect and analyze various substances with higher accuracy. However, some factors such as changes in sensor concentration, uniformity, instrument status, and environmental conditions often affect the accuracy of the detection results. In contrast, the dual emission center can overcome these challenges and enhance the sensitivity and accuracy of sensing detection by utilizing multi-dimensional signal output. In this review, our focus is on exploring the preparation techniques and intrinsic properties of CDs. In addition, we also investigated the construction process and practical applications of optical sensors based on dual emission CDs. Finally, based on the current research level and actual needs, the future development of dual emissive CDs is prospected.

In order to produce high-quality CDs in a cost-effective, simple, size-controlled, or large-scale manner, researchers have developed a variety of preparation strategies. Based on the difference in carbon sources, these strategies can be divided into top-down and bottom-up methods [16,17]. Top-down methods involve breaking down larger structures into smaller ones, while bottom-up methods involve building up from individual components. These approaches have been developed to meet the varying needs of CD preparation, and offer flexibility in achieving the desired quality and quantity of CDs.

Top-down methods in nanomaterial synthesis involve a precise and controlled fragmentation of large carbon-containing structures into smaller nanoparticles through physical or chemical processes [12]. These processes may include laser ablation, arc discharge, or plasma treatment [12]. These approaches allow for the creation of uniform CDs with customized size, shape, and surface properties at the nanoscale. By selectively decomposing larger carbon structures, the top-down method provides a high level of versatility and control over the final properties of CDs. This makes it a valuable technology for the synthesis of nanomaterials with specific and tailored characteristics. Graphene, graphene nanosheets, carbon nanotubes, and activated carbon are the precursors used for preparing CDs using the top-down method. Currently, various technologies such as electrochemical oxidation, ball milling, ultrasonic treatment, and laser ablation are employed for this purpose. Liu et al. prepared CQDs with high crystallinity by electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes in alkaline alcohol [18]. The resulting CQDs dispersion is initially colorless but gradually turns bright yellow when stored at room temperature. Furthermore, the CQDs were utilized to specifically and sensitively detect trivalent iron ions. They demonstrated the application of CQDs in detecting Fe3+ in tap water and discussed the possible mechanism. Lastly, due to their low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, CQDs were successfully used for cell imaging. The production of CDs using top-down methods allows for control over size and morphology. However, these methods require harsh experimental conditions and consume a significant amount of energy, which may not be ideal for large-scale utilization and industrial production. Additionally, CDs produced using top-down methods have a dense graphene structure and fewer surface groups, which reduces PL intensity. Despite these challenges, top-down methods show promise for producing CDs with distinct properties. Nevertheless, further research is needed to optimize the process and develop more effective and sustainable methods.

In these methods, small molecules serve as precursors for carbon sources, and organic molecules are linked to create sp2 carbon domains [5]. They are achieved through hydrothermal/solvothermal methods, microwave-assisted methods, pyrolysis methods, etc., resulting in the formation of CDs with controllable size and morphology [5]. The methods offer consistent size, adjustable structure, abundant eco-friendly materials, low cost, and simplicity. The hydrothermal/solvothermal methods, as the preferred technology for the synthesis of CDs, have received extensive attention due to their simple process and high efficiency. These methods involve using high temperature and pressure conditions in a solvent to facilitate the formation of CDs with desired properties. Song et al. used β-cyclodextrin and sodium acetate as raw materials to synthesize sodium-doped CDs (Na-CDs) using the hydrothermal method [19]. They found that Na-CDs remained stable in the pH range of 3–12 and could be preserved for an extended period. Additionally, Na-CDs exhibited high selectivity for recognizing Hg2+ in aqueous media, offering a new platform for developing PL sensors for the selective detection of Hg2+. Ganesh et al. used palm kernel shell as a raw material and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) as a solvent to synthesize CDs with an average diameter of 2.5 nm using a solvothermal method [20]. These CDs exhibited PL emission at 520 nm because of the rich functional groups on their surface. They also demonstrated excellent photo, thermal, and ionic stability. Moreover, when used as PL inks, CDs were not visible under sunlight but exhibit bright green PL emission under ultraviolet light at 365 nm. Studies have indicated that using DMF as a solvent has broadened the potential applications of these CDs in the fields of optoelectronics, charge storage systems, and electrospinning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of dual emissive materials based on CDs

ce:section.ce:para: A variety of preparation methods have been devised to modify the surfaces of CDs in order to induce dual emission. Among them, amide modification is the most effective method to generate dual emissive CDs. In Fig. 2A, Yan et al. developed a new ratiometric PL probe for visual detection of nitrogen dioxide [21]. The probe was made up of blue emitting CDs and red emitting CdTe quantum dots (QDs) through an amidation reaction, which has two emission bands with center wavelengths of 460 nm and 665 nm. The study showed that the blue emission of CDs was not affected by the analyte, while the red emission of QDs was exactly reduced. When exposed to nitrogen dioxide, the color changes from orange to blue. Li et al. combined CQDs‑gold nanoclusters (CQDs-AuNCs) using an amidation reaction to prepare nanoprobes with dual emission PL properties for the purpose of ratio sensing of H2O2. The mechanism for sensing was based on the ability of H2O2 to diminish the red emission of AuNCs. They verified the practicality of the proposed CQDs-AuNCs nanoprobe by imaging H2O2 in living cells. Zhang et al. developed a dual mode probe using 11-mercaptoalkyl acid functionalized CDs (MUA-CDs) to detect doxorubicin (DOX) [22]. CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using m-phenylenediamine and p-aminobenzoic acid as raw materials. MUA-CDs were then obtained through an amide reaction with 11-mercaptoalkanoic acid. The probe operated through the unique electrostatic interaction and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between MUA-CDs and DOX. It has demonstrated excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and outstanding anti-interference performance for detecting DOX.

Grafting PL groups on the surface of CDs is also a modification strategy for preparing dual emissive probe. For example, He et al. prepared a CDs-based ratiometric PL sensor by grafting PL groups (rhodamine B, RhB) onto CDs, as shown in Fig. 2B [23]. The dual emission peaks showed a linear ratiometric response to changes in solvent polarity and hydrogen bond of the Hansen solubility parameter. The response behavior mentioned above was linked to the surface state PL mechanism. Additionally, the CDs-based ratiometric PL sensor was utilized to quantitatively detect water content in ethanol. Furthermore, a paper-based ratiometric PL sensing band was demonstrated, showing good storage stability and sensitivity. Hu et al. reported a controlled and effective strategy for the preparation of CDs with dual PL peaks, which involved the use of coal-based CDs as seeds and the use of appropriate diammonium phosphate (DAP) as modifier [24]. The formation of dual PL color CDs (D-CDs) mainly depended on the reaction temperature and the thermally induced surface modification of coal-based CDs by DAP. The dual peak emission in PL was caused by the presence of grafted N and P-related groups on the CDs through DAP functionalization. The obtained D-CDs showed unique pH-sensitive behavior under optimized laboratory conditions. Therefore, PL ratiometric probes based on these CDs were developed to detect pH distribution and wide range of changes in complex systems with interfering metal ions.

Some simple in-situ selective self-assembly strategies have been used for obtaining CDs based on dual emission probes. For instance, Yi et al. prepared CDs-chelated Eu3+@metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) as dual emission ratiometric PL probes [25]. This probe come with an effective self-calibration function and show high sensitivity and selectivity for calcium pyridine dicarboxylate (CaDPA) in water and human serum samples. In this experimental arrangement, CDs emitting blue PL emission were used as a reference, while Eu-MOFs exhibited intensified red PL signals as a result of their interaction with the CaDPA chromophore. The sensing mechanism was attributed to the ability of CaDPA to enhance the intrinsic PL emission of Eu3+ via energy transfer between CaDPA and Eu3+.

Metal dopants are crucial for the development of CDs with dual emission capabilities. The interaction between metal elements and unpaired electrons on the surface of CDs obtains new energy gaps through intersystem crossing, leading to the inherent dual emission response in CDs. Due to the PL properties of rare earth metals (such as Tb3+ and Eu3+), they have attracted attention for combining them with CDs to form nanocomposites with dual energy bands. Zhang and colleagues produced CDs-Eu3+ hybrid composites using the hydrothermal method [26]. These composites exhibited unique dual PL emission, with the blue emission coming from CDs and the intrinsic PL of Eu3+. This dual emission characteristic showed distinct responses in different pH environments, making it suitable for use as a ratiometric pH sensor. Additionally, by co-doping Tb3+, the researchers were able to achieve white light emission, with the color temperature being adjustable by modifying the relative proportion of Eu3+.

Additionally, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ is also often used to construct dual emission probes with CDs. In Fig. 2C, Liu et al. developed a method for the instrument-free, on-site, real-time, sensitive, and visual rapid detection of fluoride (F−) content [27]. They obtained a smartphone sensing platform based on a ratiometric PL probe (AA-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+) for detecting F−. The reference signal was the red PL bipyridine ruthenium [Ru(bpy)3]2+ molecule, and the response signal was AA-CDs with Al3+ aggregation-induced enhanced emission. The PL response of the probe to different concentrations of F− varies continuously from red to cyan. There was a linear relationship between the blue-red ratio (B/R) and the concentration of F−, and the detection limit was much lower than the allowable content of F− in drinking water specified by the World Health Organization. The platform enabled rapid on-site detection of F− content in water and toothpaste samples with satisfactory reproducibility and relative standard deviation. It was characterized by low cost, portability, easy operation, good stability, good selectivity and repeatability. Zhan et al. developed a method to detect nitrite using m-CDs [28]. This method relied on the interaction between the amine group of m-CDs and nitrite, leading to the production of diazonium salt through diazotization reaction and resulting in the quenching of PL intensity of m-CDs. Additionally, they prepared a ratiometric PL probe (m-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+) by introducing Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O as an internal reference PL reagent. With the increase of nitrite concentration, the PL emission color of the ratiometric probe changed from cyan to red, which could realize visual observation. Therefore, a portable detection box based on ratiometric PL was prepared and applied to the on-site evaluation of nitrite content.

Generally, the preparation, modification and coupling process of the above dual emission probes based on CDs are complicated. Therefore, people have tried to establish a convenient method for preparing dual emission nanosensors. Recently, researchers have begun to design and develop intrinsic dual emissive CDs without external modification. Shangguan et al. introduced a new type of label-free CDs for intracellular ratiometric pH sensing [29]. These CDs had dual emission bands at 475 and 545 nm when subjected to single-wavelength excitation, and they were obtained through a simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment involving citric acid and basic fuchsin.

The PL intensity of these CDs at the two emission peaks was found to be sensitive to changes in pH. When placed in a buffer solution, the ratio of the two emission peaks showed a linear correlation with pH values ranging from 5.2 to 8.8. This property illustrated these CDs suitable for use as a ratiometric probe for intracellular pH sensing. It was worth noting that CDs show excellent reversibility and photostability in pH measurement. Song et al. developed functional CDs with dual emission using a one-step hydrothermal carbonization method [30]. When excited at 380 nm, CDs showed two distinct PL emission peaks at 440 nm and 624 nm. Unlike traditional CDs, this probe demonstrated a unique dual response function based on wavelength for detecting lysine (at 440 nm) and pH (at 624 nm), allowing for the ratiometric detection of these two targets. Quantitative analysis indicated that this ratio strategy enabled easy detection of lysine, a capability not previously reported in studies utilizing other CDs as probes. An excitation wavelength-independent red dual emission CDs were reported by Pan et al., using L-glutamic acid, o-phenylenediamine and urea as precursors and used to detect Cu2+ in vitro and in vivo [31]. Yang et al. prepared a dual emission CDs with high stability [32]. A novel ratiometric PL probe for the detection of thiabendazole (TBZ) residues was proposed, which has a wide linear range and a low detection limit. Due to the inner filter effect, the emission at 512 nm shows a special ‘turn-off’ PL sensing of TBZ, while the emission at 361 nm was almost unchanged, which could be used as a reference. In addition, the ratiometric PL strategy was successfully applied to the determination of TBZ in fruits with good recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and optical properties of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, researchers have carried out a lot of research on developing new preparation strategies, improving the performance of CDs, and exploring the complex formation and PL mechanism of CDs. This continuous attention to refinement and exploration has led to the key role of CDs as an important part of the extended domain of functional carbon nanomaterials. As described in the previous section, CDs are mainly divided into GQDs, CQDs, CPDs, CNDs and CNQDs. The structure of GQDs is characterized by one or several different graphene layers constituting a carbon core with some functional groups at their edges, with a horizontal size usually larger than their height. The morphology of CQDs is spherical, with a graphite lattice inside and different functional groups on the surface. In comparison, CNDs also have a spherical structure, but the difference is that the carbon core consists of amorphous carbon with no obvious lattice. It is worth noting that in 2019, Yang's research group proposed the concept of CPDs [11]. CPDs are nanoparticles of polymerized or crosslinked polymers, with a cross-linked polymer framework and a small amount of graphitic carbon. The characteristics of CPDs come from rich functional groups or polymer chains. CNQDs have 2D structures like graphene and, are often considered analogous to GQDs, so they are rarely discussed separately.

CDs are composed mainly of C, H, and O. The presence of carboxyl groups and amino hydroxyl groups on the surface makes them water-soluble and allows for further functionalization. Different elements are added to the CDs to diversify their composition and allow the adjustment of the conduction/valence band position and unexpected functions. N doping, originally used to improve the optical properties of CDs, is just one example. Other elements like F, P, S, B, as well as metals like Fe, Cu, Mg, and Zn can also be used in the doping of CDs to adjust their emission properties [16,33–36].

The structure of CDs can be characterized using various strategies. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are commonly used for this purpose. TEM helps to calculate the average size distribution of CDs. However, for the five classifications of CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 , they are difficult to distinguish from TEM. Usually, high-resolution TEM or further testing techniques are needed to distinguish them. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) provides height information from the images. Additionally, some techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy are used to study the crystal properties of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) can determine the elemental composition and species formation in CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) or nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) are used to further confirm the surface functional groups of CDs.

CDs often display varying absorption behaviors, depending on the carbon sources used or the methods of preparation. However, CDs usually exhibit strong absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region, and their absorption tails extend to the visible light range. The absorption band is attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond or the n-π* transition of the C=O/C=N bond [41]. For some CDs with long-wavelength emission, there are usually π-conjugated electrons in the sp2 domain and/or connected surface groups/polymer chains, leading to absorption in the 500–800 nm range [42]. In summary, the absorption properties of CDs are mainly affected by the type and content of surface groups, the size of the π-conjugated domain, and the change in N/O content in the carbon core.

PL is one of the most appealing characteristics of CDs from both theoretical and practical standpoints [43]. CDs are recognized for their advantages over traditional PL materials such as QDs, rare earth nanomaterials, and organic dyes, as they offer superior light stability, higher quantum yield (QY), lower toxicity, abundant low-cost sources, and excellent biocompatibility [44]. Therefore, CDs have important applications in various fields. In addition, CDs exhibit interesting PL emission, including excitation-dependent and excitation-independent PL, which is attributed to core-related and surface-state-related emission. The QY is a crucial parameter for characterizing the PL properties of CDs [45]. CDs usually have very low QY in early preparation. In order to improve QY, various studies have been carried out to explore the changes in reaction methods and conditions, element doping, metal-enhanced PL, and surface passivation/modification [46–48]. These developments expand the potential applications of CDs across different fields.

Since the discovery of CDs, researchers have begun to explore the PL mechanism of CDs. The study of the PL mechanism is of great significance for designing CDs with tunable PL emission. Up to now, although many studies on the PL mechanism of CDs have been reported, there is still some controversy about the origin of PL. CDs prepared by different components and methods usually contain different compositions and complex structures. CDs prepared with various preparation methods, precursors, and post-treatments show different optical properties, which indicates that the formation of CDs is a more complicated than expected. Therefore, it is inappropriate to establish a unified theory by comparing the properties of CDs reported in different literature. At present, there are some generally accepted emissive centers, including surface states, carbon core state, molecule state and the crosslink enhanced emission effect of polymer precursor [41,49]. The surface state is the earliest proposed PL mechanism of CDs, which controls the electronic structure and energy level of CDs through surface functional groups. The carbon core state is a widely used PL mechanism, especially for CDs such as CQDs and GQDs, and it refers to the fluorescence dominated by quantum confinement effect or size effect. The molecular state emphasizes that the PL emission of CDs is dominated by molecular fluorophores. The crosslink enhanced emission effect is that the cross-linking leads to the weakening of the relaxation processes such as vibration and rotation of the PL emission center, which reduces the non-radiative transition of the excited state electrons and enhances the fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of CDs in dual emissive probes
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 summarizes the examples of dual-emissive nanoprobes based on CDs for their PL properties, role, detection target, linear range and LOD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual emission composites based on CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Based on the ratiometric PL probe developed by CDs, CDs are most used as a reference signal, and the response signals include, such as AuNCs, QDs. Their most common applications are for the detection of metal cations. For example, Tan et al. reported the development of a ratiometric PL nanoprobe to detect Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 4A [50]. In this nanoprobe, CDs and AuNCs were incorporated into a material called ZIF-8 to obtain CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. These nanocomposites exhibited dual emission peaks when excited by ultraviolet light. Upon the addition of Cu2+, the PL of AuNCs was quenched, while there was no change in the PL emission of CDs. Based on this difference in PL response, CDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were utilized as ratiometric PL nanoprobes for the sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+. The dual emission probe with CDs as a reference signal is also used to detect pH. Han et al. prepared Fe2+-doped CDs at 80 °C [51]. The prepared Fe2+-doped CDs showed bright blue PL emission at 455 nm and showed good stability. Furthermore, the blue cysteine-functionalized Fe2+-doped CDs were combined with red AuNCs to form nanohybrids through electrostatic interaction. This approach was employed for the proportional PL sensing of Zn2+ and histidine (His), with the CDs functioning as reference signals and AuNCs serving as analyte response units. Two different color changes were observed in PL emission to demonstrate the detection of Zn2+ and His: a change from blue to pink and a change from pink to blue to pink, respectively. These changes exhibited outstanding selectivity and sensitivity. The dual emission PL probe addresses the constraints associated with a single PL signal, which may be susceptible to influence by experimental conditions. This technique holds great potential for practical analysis and can be used to visualize Zn2+ and His. In Fig. 4B, Yan et al. developed a new dual emission ratiometric PL sensor for biogenic amines by mixing blue PL emissive CDs and yellow PL emissive QDs [52]. QDs and CDs were used as response and internal reference signals, respectively, based on their different sensitivities to pH. In addition, copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) are also used to form nanohybrids with CDs to construct dual emission nanoprobes. Bai et al. reported a simple method for ratiometric detection of Cr2O7 2− and Cd2+ based on CDs-CuNCs nanohybrids [63]. They selected glutathione as raw material to obtain CDs (GSH@CDs), which were further used as the template to prepare CDs-stabilized CuNCs (GSH@CDs-CuNCs) that showed two well-separated emission peaks at 450 nm and 750 nm, respectively. GSH@CDs-CuNCs could be applied for the ratiometric detection of Cr2O7 2− and Cd2+ based on the PL quenching and PL enhancement of GSH@CDs-CuNCs at 750 nm.

In ratiometric PL probes based on CDs, the CDs can serve as the response signal. The reference signal is typically MOFs, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ or another type of fluorophore. They are capable of detecting metal cations and organic molecules. In Fig. 5A, Wang et al. reported a new PL sensor for detecting Cr(VI) [53]. They obtained a combination of nitrogen and cobalt co-doped CDs encapsulated in europium MOFs (CDs@Eu-MOFs). This sensor showed dual emission properties and remained stable in water. When exposed to Cr(VI), the PL intensity of CDs decreased while the PL intensity of Eu-MOFs continued constant. The prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity within a certain range, and were successfully applied to detect Cr (VI) in real samples. In Fig. 5B, Zhan et al. prepared m-CDs with high QY using m-phenylenediamine as a precursor [54]. Studies showed that hypochlorite might effectively quench the strong PL emission of m-CD. Based on this finding, a novel PL nanoprobe (m-CD) was developed for highly selective detection of hypochlorite. In addition, a portable agarose hydrogel solid matrix ratiometric PL nanoprobe (m-CD@[Ru(bpy)3]2+) sensor was subsequently obtained for visual on-site detection of hypochlorite using a naked-eye UV lamp, indicating its practical application potential, low cost, and excellent performance. Furthermore, hypochlorite was detected intracellularly and extracellularly by ratio imaging microscopy.

In CDs-based ratiometric PL probes, CDs are also good energy donors to show changes as response signal. In Fig. 6A, He et al. obtained a ratiometric PL probe (CDs-AuNCs) using fluorescence resonance energy transfer to detect dopamine (DA) with high selectivity, sensitivity, and reliability [55]. This probe contained dual fluorophores, with CDs serving as energy donors and AuNCs as acceptors. When subjected to a single excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the probe displayed two emission peaks at 420 nm and 610 nm. Upon the introduction of DA to the probe solution, there was a reduction in the PL peak at 610 nm, concomitant with the reappearance of blue PL intensity at 420 nm. The quantitative determination of DA concentration involved monitoring the change in ratiometric PL intensities at 420 nm and 610 nm. The developed sensing method has been effectively employed for the analysis of DA in serum samples, demonstrating exceptional recovery efficiency. In addition, Yu et al. developed a FRET ratiometric PL sensor to detect H2S in aqueous media, serum, and living cells [56]. In this sensor, CDs served as energy donors and anchor sites for the probe, providing high selectivity and sensitivity. Li et al. developed a ratiometric F− nanosensor that utilized PyB as the energy donor and CDs as the energy acceptor and support [64]. The affinity of the boron atom for F− presented a promising approach because of its high selectivity and sensitivity specifically for F−. The nanosensor demonstrated high sensitivity towards F− and displayed good selectivity over other anions tested in this study.

The intrinsic dual emission of CDs offers a new method for ratiometric PL probes. The PL of these probes originates from the CDs themselves and does not require additional modification. As a result, one emission peak serves as a reference signal, while the other serves as a response signal. In Fig. 6B, Pang et al. described a technique for producing CDs by heating a mixture of gallic acid and o-phenylenediamine in water. This process resulted in the generation of two types of light at wavelengths of 470 nm and 570 nm [57]. These CDs could detect Fe3+ based on PL quenching at 570 nm. The PL quenching caused by Fe3+ could be restored by pyrophosphate. Hence, the mixed system of CDs-Fe3+-pyrophosphate was used for the ratiometric detection of acid phosphatase. Guo et al. developed a new type of dual emission nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) for detecting Cu2+ and ClO− using 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid and glycine as starting materials [58]. NCDs exhibited outstanding anti-interference ability and selectivity towards different emissions. Additionally, there was a strong linear relationship between the PL intensity of NCD solutions and the concentration of Cu2+ and ClO−. The detection limits for Cu2+ and ClO− were very low. Moreover, the developed NCDs also demonstrated good recovery for detecting Cu2+ and ClO− in water. Consequently, they were anticipated to have broader applications in real samples. Zhang et al. successfully developed dual emission CDs by utilizing sodium citrate (SC), triethylenetetramine (TETA), and rose bengal (RB) [59]. During the process of hydrothermal treatment, the carboxyl group in SC undergoes crosslinking with the amino group in TETA, resulting in the formation of a polymer-like intermediate facilitated by an amide bond network. Under hydrothermal conditions, the RB molecule underwent a process in which it lost halogen atoms and integrated with the structure of the intermediate. Subsequent dehydration and carbonization leaded to the formation of dual emission CDs. Under UV light excitation, CDs displayed blue and green emission at 440 nm and 525 nm, respectively. These two colors had different sensitivities to hypochlorous acid (HClO), and analytical methods could be designed for quantitative detection of HClO. Clermont-Paquette reported a dual emission CDs for detecting glyphosate pesticides in water with different pH levels [60]. The prepared CDs had blue and red PL emission, which were used as a ratio for self-referential analysis. Red PL quenching was found with increasing concentration of glyphosate in solution, which was attributed to the interaction of glyphosate pesticides with the surface of CDs. Blue PL emission was unaffected and can be used as a reference for this ratio determination method. Therefore, a method for the ratiometric detection of glyphosate was developed. It is crucial for understanding cell activity to accurate pH monitoring in living cells. Sun et al. developed a new ratiometric pH nanosensor using label-free CDs (p-CDs) prepared by heating p-phenylenediamine and urea in solution [61]. When excited at a single wavelength (λex = 500 nm), p-CDs emitted light at two different wavelengths: 525 and 623 nm. In a buffer solution, the ratiometric of PL intensities (I525/I623) was directly linked to pH in the range of 4.0 to 8.8, making it suitable for pH sensing. The p-CDs demonstrated excellent selectivity, reversibility, and photostability in pH measurement. These properties enabled effective monitoring of pH in HeLa cells using the nanoprobe. Dual emission probes based on CDs were used for drug detection. Zhang et al. synthesized dual emissive CDs (DECDs) through a one-step solvothermal treatment of m-aminophenol and citric acid [62]. In the study, it was observed that DECDs exhibited two PL peaks at 340 nm and 420 nm when excited at a wavelength of 306 nm. In the presence of propafenone hydrochloride (PPF), the PL signal at 340 nm experienced attenuation, while the signal at 420 nm exhibited gradual enhancement, demonstrating a linear correlation with the concentration of PPF. A straightforward and efficient ratiometric PL method was developed for the determination of PPF using DECDs as probes. The analysis of PPF content yielded successful results when conducted on commercial tablets and complex biological samples. The satisfactory recovery rate demonstrated the significant potential for application in clinical research. Chen et al. used lysine (Lys) as a co-precursor and regulator to prepare CDs with adjustable dual emission by adjusting the polymerization and carbonization process of o-phenylenediamine [65]. It was found that the self-polymerization/carbonization of o-phenylenediamine contributed to the blue/green emission of CDs. The introduction of Lys during the preparation of CDs effectively inhibited the carbonization of the polymer chain and enhanced the self-polymerization of o-phenylenediamine molecules. Meanwhile, the formed o-phenylenediamine-Lys co-polymer chain give the final CDs a new green emission center. The dual emissive CDs were significantly sensitive to polarity fluctuations, providing a ratiometric PL response to the polarity of the solution. Due to its specific distribution in the endoplasmic reticulum, the prepared dual emissive CDs successfully distinguished the polarity changes of the endoplasmic reticulum under stress, providing a new way for the early diagnosis of cell damage. Li et al. prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) for ratiometric PL determination of Hg2+ [66]. Under excitation light, N, S-CDs had two emission peaks at 390 and 500 nm. It was worth noting that the presence of Hg2+ resulted in a significant decrease in PL emission of N and S-CDs at 500 nm. In contrast, the PL emission at 390 nm was almost unchanged. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed to provide a reliable, environmentally friendly, fast and efficient Hg2+ detection platform for environmental applications.

CDs have garnered significant attention for their potential as PL nanomaterials. They have shown great promise in optical detection, and recent advancements have led to the emergence of CDs-based dual emission ratiometric probes. These probes aim to further expand the application of CDs in more complex environments. However, despite these advancements, there are still numerous challenges and unresolved issues surrounding the practical application of CDs.

(1) Due to the complexity of controlling the PL emission characteristics, the direct preparation of CDs with inherent dual emission characteristics by one-step method faces major challenges. The ratiometric PL probes developed based on CDs are mostly obtained through post-modification as the main strategy. Meanwhile, the current methods for obtaining dual emissive CDs are mainly based on empirical knowledge and lack a comprehensive theoretical framework to effectively guide the process. In the future, the integration of advanced computer technology is expected to explain the basic principles of the dual emission properties of CDs, thus providing a systematic method for their synthesis and application.

(2) In the wide application of PL technology, although ratiometric PL probes have shown their unique advantages, the convenience, real-time performance and popularity of these systems still need to be improved in practical applications, especially in non-laboratory environments. In view of this, it is undoubtedly revolutionary to develop a standardized dual emissive PL detection system based on CDs, which enables real-time monitoring based on color transformation through a highly popular smartphone platform.

(3) Most of the reported dual emissive CDs have one or two PL emission centers in a shorter wavelength region, which is susceptible to background PL and light scattering in complex biological environments. Furthermore, this limits the in-depth application of dual emissive CDs in biomedical imaging, sensing and diagnosis. In order to overcome the limitation, the development of dual emissive CDs with near-infrared emission characteristics has become an attractive research direction, indicating a broad application prospect, and can become an important development trend in the field of nanomaterial science in the future.

The review aims to ignite greater interest in the advancement of dual emissive nanoprobes based on CDs. These nanoprobes have the potential to revolutionize various fields such as nanomedicine, bioanalysis, and materials science. By harnessing the unique properties of CDs, including their biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent optical properties, researchers can develop innovative tools for precise biomedical imaging, sensitive biosensing, and novel material applications. The potential impact of these dual emissive nanoprobes on healthcare, scientific research, and technological innovation is substantial, making them a promising area for further exploration and development.

Tianze Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yuanyuan Dong: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Gehui Pang: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Jiaxiang Zhao: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Guiling Wang: Supervision, Investigation. Zhaoqing Li: Validation, Investigation. Qiuyue Fu: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LH2023E113 and LH2022B020), National Nature Foundation Cultivation Project of the Fundamental Scientific Research Funds for Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (2024GJ02), and National College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program (202411802100X).
